description: X-ray transient in the Large Magellanic Cloud

temporal_properties.overall: extremely short very bright hard spike followed by ~800 s softer decaying tail

spectral_properties.spectral_evolution: clear hardening-to-softening: spike very hard (HR~1), tail softens (HR~0.4-0.6)

phenomenology: No persistent X-ray emission before or after the event
<10 s ultra-hard initial spike (hardness ratio ~1) with rise time 3-6 s
Peak count rate >=9.7 counts/s (frame-time limited lower bound), >=4 orders-of-magnitude increase
~25% of total flare counts concentrated in the initial spike
Strong spectral softening from spike to tail
Source located in the direction of the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC, ~50 kpc)
No gamma-ray or high-energy counterpart detected by wide-field monitors
No obvious optical counterpart within 2 arcsec localization (depth g~24.8, i~24.2)

multiwavelength.gamma_ray: no contemporaneous detections from wide-field monitors
multiwavelength.gravitational_wave: no constraints available at the time
multiwavelength.optical_counterpart: no obvious counterpart within 2 arcsec localization

ground_truth.note: both power law and blackbody provide statistically comparable fits given count statistics
ground_truth.physical_interpretation: thermal emission consistent with hot blackbody at ~1.8 keV

notes: Both power law and blackbody models yield comparable fits. Spectral evolution (hard spike to soft tail) and temporal properties are key physical constraints for model discrimination.
